site.name: ronutz

hero.title: The network and security toolbox that keeps your data on your machine.
hero.subtitle: Subnet math, DNS and email diagnostics, certificate inspection, and more, computed locally in your browser. No upload, no logging, no account required for the everyday tools.

credibility.title: Built by someone who has done the work since 1996.
credibility.body: Thirty years in enterprise networking and security, delivered as a working instructor rather than a marketing page. Certified across four platforms, so the comparisons here are made honestly, not sold.

pillars.title: Four platforms, taught and held current.

privacy.title: Why local-first matters for security work.
privacy.body: When you paste a configuration, a packet capture, or a certificate into a tool, that data often contains secrets. Here, the everyday tools run entirely in your browser. The input never leaves your device, so there is nothing to log, leak, or subpoena.

toolPreview.title: Try a tool right now.
toolPreview.body: The CIDR calculator below runs locally. Type a network and prefix to see the address range, host count, and mask. Nothing is sent anywhere.

provenance.title: Credits & sources
provenance.disclaimer: This tool is built from public vendor documentation and may not be fully accurate or current. For production use, always verify against the vendor's official documentation.

funding.title: Support this tool
funding.pitch: This tool is free and built to stay that way. If it earns a place in your workflow, you can help fund its upkeep and what comes next.

jwt.name: JWT Decoder & Verifier
jwt.blurb: Decode a JSON Web Token's header and claims, read its expiry and timing in plain language, and verify an HS256/384/512 signature with a pasted secret. Runs entirely in your browser.
jwt.runsLocally: Decoding and signature checks run locally. The token and secret never leave your browser.

errors.empty: Paste a JSON Web Token to decode.
errors.format: That does not look like a JWT. A token has three base64url parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature.
errors.header: The header is not valid. It should be base64url-encoded JSON.
errors.payload: The payload is not valid. It should be base64url-encoded JSON.

verify.label: Verify HMAC signature (HS256/384/512)
verify.invalid: Signature does not match this secret.
verify.algNone: This token is unsecured (alg: none), so there is no signature to verify.
verify.unsupportedAlg: In-browser verification covers HS256/384/512 only. {alg} is asymmetric and needs a public key.
verify.hint: The secret is used only to compute an HMAC in your browser. It is never sent anywhere.

struct.heading: Anatomy of a JWT
struct.note: The header and payload are only base64url-encoded, not encrypted, so anyone can read them. The signature is computed over the header and payload joined by a dot, and is what a verifier checks.

base64.name: Base64, Base32, Hex & Percent Codec
base64.blurb: Encode text to Base64, URL-safe Base64, Base32, hexadecimal, or percent-encoding, and decode any of them back. Tolerant of missing padding and whitespace, and it flags binary (non-UTF-8) results. Runs entirely in your browser.
base64.runsLocally: Encoding and decoding run locally. Nothing you paste leaves your browser.

decodeErrors.invalid-characters: That input contains characters outside the selected codec's alphabet.
decodeErrors.invalid-length: That input is not a valid length for the selected codec.
decodeErrors.invalid-escape: That input has a malformed percent-escape (a % not followed by two hex digits).

base64.decodedBytes: Decoded {bytes, plural, one {# byte} other {# bytes}}.
base64.notUtf8: Decoded {bytes, plural, one {# byte} other {# bytes}}, but the result is not valid UTF-8 text (likely binary).

hash.name: Hash Generator (SHA-1/256/384/512)
hash.blurb: Compute SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-384, and SHA-512 digests of any text, shown as hex and Base64, using the browser's native Web Crypto. Runs entirely in your browser.
hash.inputPlaceholder: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog
hash.runsLocally: Hashing runs locally via Web Crypto. Nothing you type leaves your browser.
hash.sha1Note: SHA-1 is broken for collision resistance and unsuitable for security (signatures, certificates). It is fine only for non-security checksums and legacy interop.

hmac.name: HMAC Generator (SHA-256/384/512)
hmac.blurb: Compute a keyed HMAC over a message with your secret key, shown as hex and Base64, via the browser's native Web Crypto. The same construction the JWT verifier uses for HS256. Your key never leaves your browser.
hmac.runsLocally: HMAC runs locally via Web Crypto. Your message and key never leave your browser.

struct.heading: How HMAC is built
struct.note: HMAC hashes the message twice. The key is XORed with a fixed inner pad before the first hash and with an outer pad before the second, and this two-pad construction is what prevents length-extension attacks. The key is first reduced to the hash's block size.

totp-hotp.name: TOTP / HOTP Generator & Validator
totp-hotp.blurb: Generate and check time-based (TOTP, RFC 6238) and counter-based (HOTP, RFC 4226) one-time passwords - the codes behind authenticator apps and hardware tokens such as FortiToken. SHA-1/256/512, configurable digits and step. Computed locally with Web Crypto; your secret never leaves your browser.
totp-hotp.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Your secret is never sent anywhere.
totp-hotp.secretError: That secret could not be decoded with the selected encoding. Check the value and the Base32 / Hex / ASCII toggle.
totp-hotp.validMatch: Match - this code is valid right now.
totp-hotp.validNoMatch: No match for the current parameters.
totp-hotp.validWindowNote: Real validators usually accept the adjacent steps too; this check allows +/-1 step of clock drift.
totp-hotp.explainNote: HMAC the 8-byte moving factor under your secret, take the low nibble of the last byte as an offset, read 4 bytes there as a 31-bit number, then take that modulo 10^digits. That is RFC 4226 dynamic truncation; TOTP just feeds the time step in as the counter.

diff.name: Text Diff
diff.blurb: Compare two blocks of text and see exactly what changed, line by line, with inline word-level highlighting on edited lines. Optionally ignore whitespace or case. Everything is computed in your browser; your text is never sent anywhere.
diff.emptyState: Paste text in both boxes to compare them.
diff.tooLarge: These inputs are too large to diff in the browser. Try comparing smaller sections.

pkce.name: OAuth PKCE Verifier & Challenge
pkce.blurb: Generate an OAuth 2.0 code_verifier and derive its S256 code_challenge, or paste your own and check it against RFC 7636's length and charset rules. The same SHA-256 base64url derivation your authorization server expects. Runs entirely in your browser.
pkce.verifierPlaceholder: Paste a code_verifier, or generate one
pkce.runsLocally: Generation and derivation run locally. Your verifier never leaves your browser.
pkce.plainNote: The plain method is discouraged. Use S256 wherever the client can compute SHA-256.

uuid.name: UUID Generator & Inspector (v4 / v7)
uuid.blurb: Generate random v4 or time-ordered v7 UUIDs, or paste any UUID to read its version, variant, and (for v7) the embedded creation timestamp. Generation uses the browser's secure random source. Runs entirely in your browser.
uuid.runsLocally: Generation and inspection run locally. Nothing is sent anywhere.
uuid.inspectPlaceholder: Paste any UUID to decode it

x509.name: X.509 Certificate Decoder
x509.blurb: Paste a PEM, base64, or hex certificate to read its subject, issuer, validity window, public key, and v3 extensions, with SHA-256 and SHA-1 fingerprints. Runs entirely in your browser.
x509.runsLocally: Decoding and fingerprints run locally. The certificate never leaves your browser.

errors.empty: Paste an X.509 certificate to decode.
errors.format: That does not look like a certificate. Paste PEM (-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----), base64, or hex.
errors.der: The bytes are not valid DER. Check that the whole certificate was pasted, including the END line.
errors.structure: This decodes as ASN.1 but is not an X.509 certificate. A certificate is a SEQUENCE of TBSCertificate, signatureAlgorithm, and signatureValue.

rev.noneDetail: No CRL or OCSP pointers are present in this certificate.
rev.note: These are the revocation endpoints named in the certificate. This tool reads them but never contacts them; the revocation status check happens in your client, not here.

sct.intro: Embedded SCTs: {n}. Each is a CT log's signed proof that it recorded this certificate; browsers require a minimum number for the certificate to be trusted.
sct.note: Signatures are shown but not verified here; verifying an SCT needs the log's public key and the precertificate.

x509.roleIntermediate: Signed by a root; signs leaf certificates
x509.trustNote: Trust flows downward: each certificate is signed by the one above, up to a root your system already trusts.

ipv6.name: IPv6 Toolkit
ipv6.blurb: Parse an IPv6 address or prefix to see its canonical (RFC 5952) and fully expanded forms, special-use classification, prefix math, an EUI-64 MAC if present, and its ip6.arpa reverse-DNS name. Runs entirely in your browser.
ipv6.runsLocally: Parsing runs locally. The address never leaves your browser.

errors.empty: Enter an IPv6 address or prefix to decode.
errors.format: That is not a valid IPv6 address. Use eight hex groups, optionally compressed with :: once (for example 2001:db8::1).
errors.prefix: The prefix length is not valid. For IPv6 it must be between 0 and 128.

ipv6.prefixNote: Bits left of the boundary are fixed by the prefix; bits to the right identify the host within it.
ipv6.conventionNote: No prefix was given, so the dashed line marks the conventional /64 split: routing prefix and subnet on the left, the 64-bit interface identifier on the right.

cidr.name: CIDR / Subnet Calculator
cidr.blurb: Break down any IPv4 CIDR block into network and broadcast addresses, usable host range, host count, and netmask. Runs entirely in your browser.
cidr.title: CIDR Calculator
cidr.description: Compute the network and broadcast addresses, usable host range, host count, and netmask for an IPv4 CIDR block. Runs locally in your browser.
cidr.runsLocally: Runs locally. Nothing is sent to any server.
cidr.bitsNote: The highlighted bits identify the network — the rest address hosts within it.

errors.empty: Enter a CIDR block to calculate.
errors.invalid: That does not look like a valid CIDR block. Try a format like 192.168.1.0/24.

modeDesc.subnet: Analyze a single IPv4 CIDR block: network, broadcast, mask, and host range.
modeDesc.vlsm: Carve variable-length subnets from a parent block to fit a list of host counts.
modeDesc.supernet: Summarize a list of prefixes into the smallest set of covering blocks.
modeDesc.overlap: Find overlaps and containment between prefixes, plus gaps within an optional scope.

supernet.label: Prefixes to aggregate
supernet.singleExtra: Adds {extra} extra addresses beyond the inputs.

overlap.label: Prefixes to check
overlap.noGaps: No gaps in the given scope.

err.ipv4: Not a valid IPv4 address. Use four octets 0-255, like 192.168.1.0.
err.octet: An octet is out of range (each must be 0-255).
err.prefix: The prefix length must be between 0 and 32.
err.tooMany: Too many entries. Trim the list and try again.
err.noReqs: Add at least one subnet requirement with a host count.
err.noPrefixes: Enter at least one prefix, one per line.

cidr.rfcNote: /31 and /32 reserve nothing: every address is usable (RFC 3021).
cidr.reservedNote: The first address is the network and the last is the broadcast; both are reserved, so usable = total − 2.

secure-headers.name: Secure Headers Analyzer
secure-headers.blurb: Paste an HTTP response and get a graded breakdown of its security headers, cookie flags, and cross-origin policy, checked against OWASP, RFC 6797, CSP Level 3, and RFC 6265bis.
secure-headers.inputPlaceholder:
HTTP/2 200
strict-transport-security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload
content-security-policy: default-src 'self'; object-src 'none'
x-content-type-options: nosniff
set-cookie: id=abc; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax
secure-headers.runsLocally: Analysis runs locally in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.

errors.empty: Paste an HTTP response or a block of headers to analyze.
errors.no-headers: No headers found. Paste a response with header lines like 'Header-Name: value'.

reasons.HSTS_MISSING: No Strict-Transport-Security header. Browsers may use plain HTTP, leaving the first request open to downgrade attacks.
reasons.HSTS_MAXAGE_ZERO: max-age is {value}, which disables HSTS. Set a positive max-age such as 31536000 (one year).
reasons.HSTS_MAXAGE_LOW: max-age is only {value} seconds, under the recommended six-month minimum.
reasons.HSTS_MAXAGE_OK: max-age is {value} seconds, acceptable but below the one-year baseline.
reasons.HSTS_MAXAGE_STRONG: max-age is {value} seconds (one year or more), the recommended baseline.
reasons.HSTS_SUBDOMAINS: includeSubDomains is set, extending HTTPS enforcement to every subdomain.
reasons.HSTS_NO_SUBDOMAINS: includeSubDomains is not set, so subdomains are not covered.
reasons.HSTS_PRELOAD: preload is set, making the host eligible for the browser HSTS preload list.
reasons.CSP_MISSING: No Content-Security-Policy. The main defence-in-depth control against cross-site scripting is absent.
reasons.CSP_HAS_DEFAULT_SRC: A default-src directive is set, giving every resource type a baseline policy.
reasons.CSP_NO_DEFAULT_SRC: No default-src directive, so resource types without their own directive are unrestricted.
reasons.CSP_HAS_OBJECT_SRC: object-src is set, restricting legacy plugin content.
reasons.CSP_HAS_BASE_URI: base-uri is set, preventing base-tag injection.
reasons.CSP_FRAME_ANCESTORS: frame-ancestors is set, the modern control against framing and clickjacking.
reasons.CSP_UNSAFE_INLINE: Uses 'unsafe-inline', which permits inline scripts or styles and largely defeats the XSS protection of CSP.
reasons.CSP_UNSAFE_EVAL: Uses 'unsafe-eval', which permits eval() and similar dynamic code execution.
reasons.CSP_WILDCARD: A wildcard '*' source allows content from any origin, weakening the policy.
reasons.CSP_RO_ABSENT: No report-only policy is set. This is optional and used to stage a CSP before enforcing it.
reasons.CSP_RO_ONLY: A report-only CSP is present but there is no enforced Content-Security-Policy. Report-only does not protect the page.
reasons.CSP_RO_WITH_ENFORCED: A report-only CSP is present alongside the enforced policy, useful for testing changes.
reasons.XCTO_MISSING: No X-Content-Type-Options. Browsers may MIME-sniff responses, risking content being run as script.
reasons.XCTO_NOSNIFF: Set to nosniff, which stops MIME-type sniffing.
reasons.XCTO_INVALID: Unexpected value '{value}'. The only valid value is nosniff.
reasons.XFO_MISSING: No X-Frame-Options. Without it, or CSP frame-ancestors, the page can be framed for clickjacking.
reasons.XFO_DENY: Set to DENY, preventing the page from being framed by any site.
reasons.XFO_SAMEORIGIN: Set to SAMEORIGIN, allowing framing only by same-origin pages.
reasons.XFO_ALLOWFROM_DEPRECATED: Uses ALLOW-FROM, which is obsolete and ignored by modern browsers. Use CSP frame-ancestors instead.
reasons.XFO_INVALID: Unexpected value '{value}'. Use DENY or SAMEORIGIN.
reasons.REFERRER_MISSING: No Referrer-Policy. The browser default applies, which may leak full URLs to other origins.
reasons.REFERRER_WEAK: Set to '{value}', which still sends the origin or more to other sites.
reasons.REFERRER_UNSAFE_URL: Set to unsafe-url, which sends the full URL, including path and query, to every destination.
reasons.PERMISSIONS_MISSING: No Permissions-Policy. Powerful features such as camera, microphone, and geolocation are not restricted.
reasons.PERMISSIONS_PRESENT: Present, restricting access to powerful browser features.
reasons.COOP_MISSING: No Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy. The page shares a browsing-context group with cross-origin openers.
reasons.COOP_SAME_ORIGIN: Set to same-origin, isolating the page from cross-origin windows.
reasons.COOP_ALLOW_POPUPS: Set to same-origin-allow-popups, isolating the page while keeping popups it opens.
reasons.COOP_UNSAFE_NONE: Set to unsafe-none, which provides no opener isolation.
reasons.COEP_ABSENT: No Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy. This is optional and required only for cross-origin isolation.
reasons.COEP_PRESENT: Set to '{value}', requiring embedded resources to opt in.
reasons.CORP_ABSENT: No Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy. This is optional and limits who may embed this resource.
reasons.CORP_SAME_ORIGIN: Set to same-origin, the tightest setting.
reasons.CORP_SAME_SITE: Set to same-site, allowing same-site embedding.
reasons.CORP_CROSS_ORIGIN: Set to cross-origin, allowing any site to embed this resource. Make sure that is intended.
reasons.CACHE_ABSENT: No Cache-Control header. Set no-store on responses that carry sensitive data.
reasons.CACHE_RESTRICTED: Caching is restricted (no-store, no-cache, or private), appropriate for sensitive responses.
reasons.CACHE_PERMISSIVE: Caching is permissive. Make sure the response contains no sensitive data.
reasons.CORS_WILDCARD: Access-Control-Allow-Origin is '*', letting any site read the response. This is safe only for public, credential-free data.
reasons.CORS_WILDCARD_CREDENTIALS: Access-Control-Allow-Origin is '*' together with credentials. This exposes credentialed data to any origin, a serious misconfiguration.
reasons.CORS_NULL_ORIGIN: Access-Control-Allow-Origin is 'null', which several contexts can forge. Avoid the null origin.
reasons.CORS_SPECIFIC_ORIGIN: Access-Control-Allow-Origin allows '{value}'. Confirm that origin is trusted.
reasons.CORS_CREDENTIALS_TRUE: Access-Control-Allow-Credentials is true, so the response may carry cookies cross-origin. Pair this only with a specific, trusted origin.
reasons.XPCDP_ABSENT: No X-Permitted-Cross-Domain-Policies. OWASP recommends setting it to none.
reasons.XPCDP_NONE: Set to none, the hardened value.
reasons.XPCDP_ALL: Set to all, the most permissive value. Prefer none.
reasons.CLEAR_SITE_DATA_PRESENT: Clear-Site-Data is set ('{value}'), telling the browser to clear site data. Use it on logout endpoints.
reasons.FEATURE_POLICY_DEPRECATED: Feature-Policy is deprecated. Migrate to Permissions-Policy.
reasons.HPKP_DEPRECATED: HTTP Public Key Pinning is deprecated and removed from browsers. Remove this header.
reasons.EXPECT_CT_OBSOLETE: Expect-CT is obsolete ('{value}'). Certificate Transparency is enforced by default, so remove it.
reasons.XSS_PROTECTION_LEGACY: X-XSS-Protection is '{value}'. The legacy auditor is gone from modern browsers. Remove it, or set it to 0, and rely on CSP.
reasons.XSS_PROTECTION_DISABLED_OK: X-XSS-Protection is 0, which correctly disables the obsolete auditor.
reasons.SERVER_VERSION_DISCLOSED: Server reveals version detail ('{value}'), helping attackers target known flaws. Minimise it.
reasons.SERVER_PRODUCT_DISCLOSED: Server names the product ('{value}'). Consider removing or minimising it.
reasons.XPOWEREDBY_PRESENT: X-Powered-By reveals the stack ('{value}'). Remove it.
reasons.XASPNETVERSION_PRESENT: X-AspNet-Version reveals the runtime version ('{value}'). Remove it.
reasons.XASPNETMVCVERSION_PRESENT: X-AspNetMvc-Version reveals the framework version ('{value}'). Remove it.
reasons.COOKIE_SECURE: Secure is set, so the cookie is sent only over HTTPS.
reasons.COOKIE_NO_SECURE: Secure is not set, so the cookie can be sent over plain HTTP.
reasons.COOKIE_HTTPONLY: HttpOnly is set, hiding the cookie from JavaScript.
reasons.COOKIE_NO_HTTPONLY: HttpOnly is not set, so script can read the cookie and an XSS flaw could steal it.
reasons.COOKIE_SAMESITE_STRICT: SameSite=Strict, the strongest cross-site request protection.
reasons.COOKIE_SAMESITE_NONE: SameSite=None with Secure, allowing cross-site sending.
reasons.COOKIE_SAMESITE_NONE_INSECURE: SameSite=None without Secure. Browsers reject this and it is insecure. Add Secure.
reasons.COOKIE_NO_SAMESITE: No SameSite attribute. Set Lax or Strict for cross-site request resistance.
reasons.COOKIE_HOST_PREFIX_OK: The __Host- prefix requirements are met (Secure, Path=/, and no Domain).
reasons.COOKIE_HOST_PREFIX_VIOLATION: The __Host- prefix requires Secure, Path=/, and no Domain. Those conditions are not all met.
reasons.COOKIE_SECURE_PREFIX_OK: The __Secure- prefix requirement is met (Secure is set).
reasons.COOKIE_SECURE_PREFIX_VIOLATION: The __Secure- prefix requires Secure, which is not set.
reasons.OVERALL_INFO_LEAK: {value} header(s) disclose server or stack details.
reasons.OVERALL_CORS_RISK: A cross-origin (CORS) policy exposes data to other sites.
reasons.OVERALL_POOR: Several core security headers are missing.

saml-decoder.name: SAML Decoder
saml-decoder.blurb: Paste a SAML Response or assertion (raw, base64, or URL-encoded) and decode its issuer, status, subject, conditions, audience, and attributes, with signature and weak-algorithm checks. Hardened against XXE.
saml-decoder.inputPlaceholder: Paste a SAML Response, an assertion, or the base64 SAMLResponse value
saml-decoder.runsLocally: Decoding runs locally in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.
saml-decoder.encryptedNote: {n} encrypted assertion(s) detected. Decrypting requires the service provider's private key and is out of scope for this decoder.
saml-decoder.notVerifiedNote: This tool decodes and explains structure only. It does not verify signatures, validate the message, or decrypt encrypted content.

errors.empty: Paste a SAML message to decode.
errors.not-decodable: This does not look like XML or base64. Paste a SAML Response, an assertion, or its base64 form.
errors.deflated: This decodes to compressed (DEFLATE) bytes, which is the HTTP-Redirect binding. Paste the raw XML or the base64 HTTP-POST form instead.
errors.doctype-forbidden: This message contains a DOCTYPE or entity declaration, which a SAML message must not have. It is rejected to prevent XML external entity (XXE) attacks.
errors.not-xml: The input could not be parsed as XML.
errors.no-saml: This is XML, but not a recognizable SAML message.
errors.malformed: The XML is malformed (an unclosed or mismatched tag).
errors.too-large: The message is too large to decode here.
errors.too-deep: The XML is nested too deeply to decode.
errors.too-many-nodes: The XML has too many elements to decode.

reasons.SIGNED_RESPONSE: The response is signed at the top level.
reasons.SIGNED_ASSERTION: An assertion signature is present ({value} signed).
reasons.UNSIGNED: Nothing in this message is signed. Its authenticity cannot be established, so it must be treated as untrusted until a signature is added and verified.
reasons.WEAK_SIG_ALGO: The signature uses {value}, a weak algorithm. Prefer RSA-SHA256 or stronger.
reasons.WEAK_DIGEST_ALGO: The signature digest uses {value}, a weak hash. Prefer SHA-256 or stronger.
reasons.ENCRYPTED_ASSERTION: {value} assertion(s) are encrypted. This tool reports their presence but does not decrypt them.
reasons.STATUS_NOT_SUCCESS: The response status is {value}, not Success. The login did not succeed.
reasons.NO_CONDITIONS: An assertion has no Conditions element, so it carries no validity window or audience restriction.
reasons.NO_AUDIENCE_RESTRICTION: An assertion has Conditions but no AudienceRestriction, so it is not bound to a specific service provider.
reasons.BEARER_NO_NOTONORAFTER: A bearer SubjectConfirmation has no NotOnOrAfter, so the confirmation does not expire. This weakens replay protection.
reasons.BEARER_NO_RECIPIENT: A bearer SubjectConfirmation has no Recipient, so it is not bound to the intended endpoint.
reasons.MULTIPLE_ASSERTIONS: The message contains {value} assertions. Confirm your service provider processes the one it expects, since multiple assertions can be used in wrapping attacks.

flow.heading: Web browser SSO (SP-initiated)
flow.s1: A user requests a protected resource at the service provider
flow.s2: The SP builds an AuthnRequest and redirects the browser to the IdP
flow.s3: The IdP receives the AuthnRequest over the Redirect binding
flow.s5: The IdP issues a signed Response carrying an Assertion
flow.s6: The browser POSTs the SAMLResponse back to the SP's ACS
flow.s7: The SP checks the signature, conditions, and audience
flow.s8: The SP establishes a session and grants access

oidc.name: OIDC Decoder
oidc.blurb: Paste an OpenID Connect ID token or a .well-known/openid-configuration document and decode it: the core claims, profile claims, endpoints, and capabilities, with checks for required claims, signing algorithm, nonce, and PKCE.
oidc.inputPlaceholder: Paste an ID token, or an OIDC discovery JSON document
oidc.runsLocally: Decoding runs locally in your browser. It never calls the jwks_uri or any endpoint, and nothing is sent anywhere.
oidc.notVerifiedNote: This tool decodes and explains. It does not verify the token signature, fetch the signing keys, or check expiry against the clock.

errors.empty: Paste an ID token or an openid-configuration document to decode.
errors.malformed-json: This starts like JSON but could not be parsed. Check for a missing brace, comma, or quote.
errors.json-not-oidc: This is JSON, but not an OpenID Connect discovery document. Paste an ID token, or a .well-known/openid-configuration document.
errors.not-jwt: This does not look like a JWT or an OIDC document. Paste an ID token or a discovery document.
errors.jwt-header: The token header could not be decoded. A JWT is three base64url parts separated by dots.
errors.jwt-payload: The token payload could not be decoded as JSON.

reasons.MISSING_REQUIRED_CLAIM: Missing required claim {value}. An ID token must contain iss, sub, aud, exp, and iat.
reasons.ALG_NONE: The token uses alg none, meaning it is unsigned. An unsigned ID token proves nothing and must be rejected.
reasons.ALG_SYMMETRIC: Signed with {value}, a symmetric (HMAC) algorithm. ID tokens from a public provider normally use an asymmetric algorithm like RS256 or ES256 so the relying party can verify with the public key.
reasons.ALG_UNKNOWN: Signed with {value}, an algorithm this tool does not recognize. Confirm it is an expected, strong algorithm.
reasons.SIGNED_ASYMMETRIC: Signed with {value}, an asymmetric algorithm. The relying party verifies it against the provider's public key from the JWKS.
reasons.NO_NONCE: No nonce claim. The nonce binds the token to the authentication request and protects against replay. It should be present when the request sent one.
reasons.MULTI_AUD_NO_AZP: The token has multiple audiences but no azp (authorized party). With more than one audience, azp should name the client the token is for.
reasons.DISCOVERY_ALG_NONE: The provider advertises the none signing algorithm for ID tokens, which would allow unsigned tokens. This should not be offered.
reasons.DISCOVERY_NO_PKCE: No code_challenge_methods_supported. The provider does not advertise PKCE, which is recommended for the authorization code flow.
reasons.DISCOVERY_NO_PKCE_S256: PKCE is advertised but without the S256 method. S256 is the recommended code challenge method.

flow.heading: Authorization code flow
flow.s1: Redirect the user to the provider with scope=openid
flow.s2: Provider authenticates the user and gets consent
flow.s3: Provider redirects back with an authorization code
flow.s4: Relying Party exchanges the code at the token endpoint
flow.s5: Provider returns an ID token plus an access token
flow.s6: Relying Party validates the ID token signature against the JWKS
flow.s7: Relying Party may call UserInfo with the access token

f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.name: BIG-IP Persistence Cookie Decoder
f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.blurb: Decode an F5 BIGipServer persistence cookie into the backend pool member's IP and port, or encode one from an address and port. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.inputPlaceholder: Paste a cookie value, a name=value pair, or a full Set-Cookie line. Example: 1677787402.20480.0000
f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.runsLocally: Decoded in your browser. The cookie is never sent anywhere.

errors.empty: Enter a BIGipServer cookie value to decode.
errors.unrecognized: This does not match any known BIG-IP persistence cookie format. Check that you pasted the cookie value, not just the name.

encodeErrors.address: Enter a valid IPv4 or IPv6 address.
encodeErrors.routedomain: Route domain must be a number between 0 and 65535.

reasons.INFO_DISCLOSURE: This cookie exposes a backend pool member's IP address and port to anyone who can read the response. Enable cookie encryption on the persistence profile to prevent this.
reasons.INTERNAL_ADDRESS: The decoded address is {value}, confirming an internal pool member and revealing part of your internal topology.
reasons.ROUTE_DOMAIN: The pool member sits in route domain {value}.
reasons.ENCRYPTED: This cookie is AES-encrypted. The pool member cannot be recovered without the BIG-IP's key, which is the recommended secure configuration.

f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.disclosureNote: Decoding uses only the public encoding scheme (F5 K6917). It confirms what any client can already read from the cookie; the fix is to encrypt the persistence cookie.
f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.encryptedNote: An encrypted persistence cookie is the recommended configuration. There is nothing to decode without the key, and that is by design.

encode.resultNote: This is the value a BIG-IP would set for that pool member using the default (unencrypted) encoding.

url-inspector.name: URL Inspector
url-inspector.blurb: Dissect any URL into its named parts: scheme, host, port, path, query parameters, and fragment. Decodes percent-escapes and internationalized hosts, and flags credentials and other issues. Runs entirely in your browser.
url-inspector.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. The URL is never sent anywhere.
url-inspector.emptyQuery: The query string is present but empty.

reasons.RELATIVE_REFERENCE: This is a relative reference, not an absolute URL: it has no scheme and no host.
reasons.NO_SCHEME: No scheme is present, so this URL is not absolute.
reasons.CREDENTIALS_IN_URL: This URL embeds a username and password. Credentials in a URL are easily logged, cached, and shared, and should be avoided.
reasons.USERINFO_PRESENT: This URL includes a userinfo component (a username before the host).
reasons.PLAINTEXT_SCHEME: The {value} scheme is not encrypted in transit. Anything sent over it can be read on the network.
reasons.NON_DEFAULT_PORT: The port {value} is not the default for this scheme.
reasons.REDUNDANT_DEFAULT_PORT: The port {value} is the default for this scheme and can be omitted.
reasons.IDN_HOST: The host is internationalized and stored in punycode; the Unicode form is shown above. Watch for look-alike characters in untrusted links.
reasons.UPPERCASE_SCHEME: The scheme contains uppercase letters. Schemes are case-insensitive and are normally written in lowercase.
reasons.UPPERCASE_HOST: The host contains uppercase letters. Host names are case-insensitive and are normally written in lowercase.
reasons.PERCENT_ENCODED: This URL contains percent-encoded characters, which have been decoded in the breakdown above.
reasons.PLUS_IN_QUERY: The query contains a plus sign. Under form encoding (application/x-www-form-urlencoded) a plus means a space; under strict RFC 3986 it is a literal plus. The values above keep it literal.

url-inspector.note: Components are shown exactly as written, without normalization. Parsing follows RFC 3986; the query is split on & and =, and each part is percent-decoded.

notes.COMMENTS_DROPPED: Comments were dropped. JSON has no way to represent them.
notes.ANCHORS_EXPANDED: Anchors and aliases were expanded inline; the repeated values are written out in full.

syslog-pri-decoder.facilityVaries: Facilities 12 to 15 are not standardized in RFC 5424 and vary by implementation.

struct.heading: How a PRI splits into facility and severity
struct.note: A single PRI integer carries both fields. Dividing by 8 gives the facility, and the remainder gives the severity, so PRI 134 is facility 16 and severity 6. Encoding runs the other way: facility × 8 + severity.

jwks-explainer.jwtInputPlaceholder: Paste a JWT, or just its header
jwks-explainer.privateBanner: This set contains private key material. A JWKS should only ever contain public keys.
jwks-explainer.algMismatch: The JWT alg does not match the matched key alg.

diag.heading: How a key is matched by kid
diag.note: When a JWT arrives, the verifier reads its kid and finds the one key in the set with the same kid, then uses that key to check the signature. Each key needs a distinct kid for this to be unambiguous.

regex.blurb: Test, explain, and debug regular expressions with live matches and a backtracking-risk check.

regex.description: Compile a JavaScript regular expression, test it against your own text with every match and capture group highlighted, and read a plain-language breakdown of what the pattern does — all in your browser.
regex.redosBody: This pattern nests an unbounded quantifier inside another (the (a+)+ shape), which can be slow or hang on some inputs. Run it against a short string first.
regex.noMatches: No matches in the test text.
regex.tooLong: The test text is too long to run; shorten it and try again.

tools.title: Tools
tools.lede: Small, fast, privacy-first utilities that run entirely in your browser. Nothing you type is sent anywhere.
tools.note: This toolbox is growing. New tools are added here as they ship, each one local-first and free to use.

cipher.name: Cipher Suite Decoder
cipher.blurb: Enter a TLS cipher suite, as an IANA name, an OpenSSL or GnuTLS name, or a hex code point, to break it into its key exchange, authentication, cipher, mode, and MAC, with a plain-language security read-out and the official IANA recommendation. Runs entirely in your browser against a bundled copy of the IANA registry.
cipher.runsLocally: Decoding runs locally against a bundled copy of the IANA TLS Cipher Suite registry. Nothing is sent anywhere.
cipher.notInRegistryNote: This name parsed structurally but was not found in the bundled IANA registry snapshot, so its code point and cross-names are not shown.

errors.empty: Enter a cipher suite to decode.
errors.format: That is not a cipher suite. Enter an IANA name (TLS_...), an OpenSSL name, or a hex code point such as 0x1301.
errors.unknown: That is a valid code-point format, but it is not an assigned cipher suite in the IANA registry.

reasons.nullCipher: NULL cipher: the traffic is not encrypted at all.
reasons.anon: Anonymous key exchange: the peer is not authenticated, so it gives no protection against an active man-in-the-middle.
reasons.export40: Export-grade 40-bit key: trivially brute-forced, a relic of 1990s export rules.
reasons.rc4: RC4 stream cipher: a biased keystream makes it broken, and RFC 7465 prohibits it for TLS.
reasons.singleDes: Single DES with a 56-bit key: brute-forceable in hours.
reasons.rc2: RC2: an obsolete 40/64-bit cipher that is not safe for modern use.
reasons.tripleDes: 3DES has a 64-bit block, which exposes it to the Sweet32 birthday attack (CVE-2016-2183). RFC 8429 deprecates it.
reasons.md5Mac: MD5 MAC: MD5 is cryptographically broken.
reasons.cbcMac: CBC with an HMAC-SHA1 or MD5 MAC uses MAC-then-encrypt, which is exposed to padding-oracle attacks such as BEAST and Lucky13.
reasons.ccm8: CCM_8 truncates the authentication tag to 8 bytes, weakening integrity. IANA does not mark it Recommended.
reasons.noFs: No forward secrecy: with a static key exchange, one compromised long-term key can decrypt past sessions. Modern guidance requires ECDHE or DHE.
reasons.legacyCipher: {value} is a legacy cipher that is deprecated for TLS.
reasons.aead: AEAD cipher (authenticated encryption): confidentiality and integrity in one primitive, with no separate MAC to misuse.
reasons.fs: Forward secrecy: an ephemeral key exchange keeps past traffic safe even if the long-term key is later compromised.
reasons.tls13: TLS 1.3 suite: only modern AEAD ciphers, with the key exchange negotiated separately and always ephemeral.
reasons.ianaD: IANA marks this suite "D" (discouraged): it SHOULD NOT or MUST NOT be used, depending on the situation.
reasons.ianaN: IANA does not mark this suite "Recommended": it has limited applicability or has not been through the IETF consensus process.
reasons.signalling: Signalling suite (SCSV): not an actual cipher, it carries a protocol signal such as fallback protection or renegotiation info.

groups.title: Key-exchange groups
groups.intro: TLS negotiates the actual key-agreement group separately from the cipher suite, in the supported_groups extension. As "harvest now, decrypt later" drives the move to post-quantum key agreement, the hybrid groups below pair a classical curve with ML-KEM.
groups.foot: X25519MLKEM768 is the hybrid most browsers now send by default.

f5-ssl-profile-explainer.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.scopeNote: Decodes a pasted profile block. It never contacts a BIG-IP.
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.err_empty: Paste an SSL profile to begin.
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.err_noHeader: Expected a line like: ltm profile client-ssl /Common/name ...
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.err_noBody: No opening brace was found in the profile.
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.err_tooLong: That input is too large to parse.
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.err_invalid: That does not look like a client-ssl or server-ssl profile.
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.clientNote: A client-ssl profile terminates the client's TLS at the BIG-IP. The connection on to the pool member is re-encrypted only if a server-ssl profile is applied; with none, that leg is cleartext (SSL offload).
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.serverNote: A server-ssl profile lets the BIG-IP open a fresh TLS connection to the pool member (re-encryption). The client side is handled by a separate client-ssl profile.

cert-renewal-planner.name: Certificate renewal planner
cert-renewal-planner.blurb: Work out a TLS certificate's validity, whether it fits the CA/Browser Forum 47-day schedule, and the renewal cadence it implies — all offline.
cert-renewal-planner.runsLocally: Everything runs in your browser; nothing is uploaded.
cert-renewal-planner.maxForPhase: Maximum for its issuance date: {max} days.
cert-renewal-planner.overByText: {days} days over the {max}-day maximum for its issuance date — a public CA would not issue it.
cert-renewal-planner.renewalsValue: About {n} renewals per year at this validity.
cert-renewal-planner.dcvText: Domain validation (DCV) data can be reused for {days} days.
cert-renewal-planner.siiText: Organization identity (OV/EV SII) can be reused for {days} days.
cert-renewal-planner.scheduleIntro: Public TLS maximums key off the certificate's issuance date:
cert-renewal-planner.projectionIntro: Renewals per certificate per year, as the caps tighten:
cert-renewal-planner.note_overCap: This validity exceeds the {max}-day maximum for its issuance date.
cert-renewal-planner.note_future47: Compliant today, but longer than the eventual 47-day cap — expect more frequent renewals as the schedule advances.
cert-renewal-planner.note_publicOnly: These caps apply to publicly trusted TLS certificates only. Private or internal PKI is not governed by SC-081v3.
cert-renewal-planner.note_automate: At these cadences, automate issuance and renewal with ACME (RFC 8555) and ARI (RFC 9773); manual renewal does not scale.
cert-renewal-planner.err_empty: Enter both the issue and expiry dates.
cert-renewal-planner.err_order: The expiry date must be after the issue date.

csr-decoder.err_empty: Paste a certificate signing request to decode.
csr-decoder.err_format: That does not look like a PEM, base64, or hex CSR.
csr-decoder.err_der: The DER structure could not be parsed.

f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.name: BIG-IP tcpdump builder
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.blurb: Assemble a BIG-IP-correct tcpdump command from structured choices: the TMM interface syntax, flow detail, snaplen, file output, and a BPF filter. It formats the command for you to run; it captures nothing.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.runsLocally: The command is assembled in your browser. Nothing is captured, run, or sent anywhere.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.ifaceHint: 0.0 captures on every TMM. You can also name a VLAN, a tunnel, or a self-IP.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.detailHint: Adds the F5 noise/flow trailer to each packet. Higher detail prints flow and peer-flow information.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.bothSidesHint: Capture the client-side and server-side flows of the same connection.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.nameResHint: -n and -nn keep the capture fast and avoid reverse lookups that can stall it.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.countHint: Optional. Leave blank to capture until you stop it with Ctrl-C.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.fileNameHint: Required when sending a capture to F5 Support. Save under /var/tmp or /shared/tmp.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.filterHint: Standard tcpdump/BPF syntax: host, net, port, src/dst, tcp/udp, and / or / not.

warnings.unfiltered-all-tmm: Capturing on 0.0 is not rate-limited. Add a filter (host or port) or a packet count (-c) so the capture file stays bounded.
warnings.snaplen-zero-modern: On BIG-IP 15.x and later, -s0 maps to the default snap length of 262144 bytes (kept for compatibility), so it still captures whole packets.
warnings.hex-with-writefile: -X only affects on-screen output. It has no effect when you also write a binary file with -w.
warnings.colon-in-iface-name: An interface name must not contain a colon on BIG-IP. The colon is reserved for the TMM detail suffix.

dig-output-explainer.name: dig output explainer
dig-output-explainer.blurb: Paste real dig output and get a decoded, explained breakdown: the header and flags, the EDNS OPT pseudo-section, every record in each section, and the query stats. Parsed entirely in your browser, nothing is resolved or sent anywhere.

input.label: Paste dig output
input.placeholder:
; <<>> DiG 9.18.1 <<>> example.com A
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: ...

dig-output-explainer.privacy: Parsed entirely in your browser. Nothing is resolved or sent anywhere.
dig-output-explainer.notDig: This does not look like dig output. Paste the full text dig prints, including the ;; ->>HEADER<<- line.

warnings.title: Worth noticing
warnings.nonzero-rcode: The status is not NOERROR, so this is not a normal successful answer. See the status meaning above.
warnings.truncated-tc: The TC (truncated) flag is set: the UDP answer did not fit and is incomplete. dig normally retries over TCP.
warnings.noerror-nodata: Status is NOERROR but there are no answer records (NODATA): the name exists, but not for this record type.
warnings.looks-like-query: The QR flag is not set, so this looks like a query rather than a response.
warnings.dnssec-requested-not-validated: DNSSEC was requested (the DO flag is set) but the AD flag is not, so the resolver did not return validated data.

opt.title: EDNS (OPT pseudo-section)
opt.help: EDNS(0) extends DNS over UDP: it advertises a larger UDP payload size and carries flags such as DO (DNSSEC OK) and options like COOKIE.

nslookup-output-explainer.name: nslookup output explainer
nslookup-output-explainer.blurb: Paste real nslookup output and get a decoded, explained breakdown: the resolver it used, whether the answer is authoritative, every record (with MX / SRV / SOA field breakdowns), and any failures. Parsed entirely in your browser, nothing is resolved or sent anywhere.

input.label: Paste nslookup output
input.placeholder:
Server:	1.1.1.1
Address:	1.1.1.1#53

Non-authoritative answer:
Name:	example.com
Address: 93.184.216.34

nslookup-output-explainer.privacy: Parsed entirely in your browser. Nothing is resolved or sent anywhere.
nslookup-output-explainer.notNslookup: This does not look like nslookup output. Paste the full text nslookup prints, including the Server: line.

warnings.title: Worth noticing
warnings.nxdomain: The server reports the name does not exist (NXDOMAIN).
warnings.servfail: The server could not complete the query (SERVFAIL), often a broken delegation, a DNSSEC failure, or an upstream timeout.
warnings.refused: The server refused the query (REFUSED), usually a policy or access-control decision.
warnings.no-servers: No server could be reached (a timeout). Check the resolver address and connectivity.
warnings.non-authoritative: This answer is non-authoritative: it came from the resolver's cache, not directly from a server authoritative for the zone. That is normal for a recursive lookup.

server.title: Resolver used
server.help: This is the DNS server nslookup queried, not the answer. Its address includes the port (#53).

authority.title: Answer authority
authority.nonAuthHelp: The answer came from the resolver's cache. To get an authoritative answer, query a server listed in the zone's NS records directly.

referral.note: nslookup listed the authoritative servers it would refer you to for a definitive answer.

xml-decoder.name: XML Decoder
xml-decoder.blurb: Paste XML and read its structure: the declaration, the DOCTYPE and any entities, the element tree with namespaces and attributes, plus a security check of the XML attack surface. Nothing is fetched or resolved.
xml-decoder.privacy: Parsed entirely in your browser. No entity is resolved, no external reference is fetched, and nothing is uploaded.
xml-decoder.notXml: This does not look like XML. Paste a document that starts with an element or an XML declaration.

security.title: Security analysis
security.doctype-present: A DOCTYPE is present. The DOCTYPE is the gateway to XML entity attacks; a hardened parser disables it entirely.
security.external-dtd: The DOCTYPE references an external DTD. A parser that fetches it can be steered to attacker-controlled content.
security.external-entity: An external entity is declared (SYSTEM or PUBLIC). Resolving it would read a local file or make a network request: the classic XXE vector.
security.parameter-entity: A parameter entity is declared. Parameter entities are used to build blind and out-of-band XXE payloads.
security.entity-expansion: Entities reference other entities. Chained expansion can grow exponentially and exhaust memory, the billion laughs denial of service.

errors.unexpected-close: Closing tag with no matching open: {detail}
errors.multiple-roots: Multiple root elements ({detail}). XML must have exactly one.
errors.unterminated-cdata: A CDATA section was never closed.

f5xc-service-policy-explainer.name: F5 XC Service Policy Explainer
f5xc-service-policy-explainer.blurb: Paste an F5 Distributed Cloud service_policy and see exactly how it matches: the server scope, the rule order, and every rule's action and conditions. iRules for XC, decoded.

input.label: F5 XC service_policy JSON
input.placeholder: Paste a service_policy spec (Create, Replace, or Get form, or a bare spec)...

f5xc-service-policy-explainer.privacy: Decoded locally in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere, and the policy is never evaluated against live traffic.
f5xc-service-policy-explainer.notPolicy: This does not look like an F5 XC service_policy. Expected a spec with a rule choice (rule_list, allow_all_requests, allow_list, deny_list, ...) or a server choice.

warnings.title: Worth a look
warnings.allow-all-policy: This policy allows all requests. Every request that reaches it is permitted, with no further checks.
warnings.allow-all-rule: A rule allows every request (ALLOW with no conditions). It matches everything that reaches it, so any rule below it is never evaluated. This is the common catch-all pattern, but confirm it is intentional.
warnings.no-rules: The rule list is empty. With no rules to match, requests fall through and the default is to deny.
warnings.expired-rule: A rule has an expiration timestamp in the past. It still exists in the config but is no longer applied.
warnings.case-sensitive-exact: An exact-value matcher has no LOWER_CASE or UPPER_CASE transformer, so the comparison is case-sensitive. '/Admin' will not match '/admin'.
warnings.inverted-matcher: A matcher is inverted (invert_matcher). It matches when the condition is NOT met, which is easy to misread.

algorithm.title: How the rules are evaluated
algorithm.FIRST_MATCH: First match: rules are evaluated top to bottom until one matches. That rule's action is applied and evaluation stops.
algorithm.DENY_OVERRIDES: Deny overrides: all DENY rules are evaluated before any ALLOW rule, so a matching DENY wins over a matching ALLOW.
algorithm.ALLOW_OVERRIDES: Allow overrides: all ALLOW rules are evaluated before any DENY rule, so a matching ALLOW wins over a matching DENY.
algorithm.defaultDeny: If no rule matches, the request does not match this policy and the next policy in the set is evaluated. If nothing allows it, the request is denied.

rule.matchesEverything: No conditions: this rule matches every request that reaches it.

logic.andAll: All of the following must be true (AND):

sourceList.title: Source list
sourceList.allow_list: A request is allowed if its source matches any of the following:
sourceList.deny_list: A request is denied if its source matches any of the following:

legacy.title: Referenced rules (legacy)
legacy.note: This policy points to separate service_policy_rule objects rather than defining rules inline. Their contents are not part of this spec.

cvss-vector-decoder.name: CVSS vector decoder
cvss-vector-decoder.blurb: Paste a CVSS v3.1 or v3.0 vector and get the score computed and mapped to a severity, with every metric spelled out. Local and offline; nothing is sent anywhere.
cvss-vector-decoder.privacy: The vector is scored in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
cvss-vector-decoder.notCvss: Paste a CVSS v3.0 or v3.1 vector string to decode it.
cvss-vector-decoder.parseError: That does not parse as a CVSS vector ({detail}).
cvss-vector-decoder.unsupportedVersion: This is a CVSS {version} vector. This decoder computes CVSS v3.0 and v3.1 only. CVSS v4.0 uses a different scoring model, and CVSS v2 is retired.

warnings.title: Notes
warnings.no-version-prefix: No CVSS version prefix was found. Scored as CVSS v3.1; add a CVSS:3.1/ or CVSS:3.0/ prefix to be explicit.
warnings.duplicate-metric: A metric appears more than once. The last value given was used.
warnings.incomplete-base: Some mandatory base metrics are missing, so no score can be computed. All eight of AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, and A are required.

http-request-translator.name: HTTP request translator
http-request-translator.blurb: Paste a curl command and get it explained flag by flag, then translated to fetch, a raw HTTP request, HTTPie, and Python requests. Local and offline; the command is decoded in your browser and nothing is sent or run.
http-request-translator.privacy: Parsed locally in your browser. A curl command can carry tokens, cookies, or passwords, so nothing you paste is sent anywhere or executed.

err.empty: Paste a curl command to begin.
err.notCurl: This does not look like a curl command. Paste a command that starts with curl.
err.noUrl: No URL found. curl needs a target URL.

opt.head: Send a HEAD request (headers only, no body).
opt.get: Force GET and move data into the query string.
opt.include: Include the response headers in the output.
opt.verbose: Verbose output (show the request and response).
opt.proxy: Send the request through a proxy:
opt.output: Write the response body to a file:
opt.max-time: Maximum time for the whole operation, in seconds:
opt.connect-timeout: Maximum time allowed for the connection, in seconds:
opt.range: Request only part of the resource (byte range):
opt.retry: Retry the request on transient failures, up to:
opt.remote-name: Save the response using the remote file name.
opt.fail: Fail silently on server errors (no error-page body).

warnings.insecureTls: -k / --insecure disables TLS certificate verification. The connection is encrypted but not authenticated, so it is exposed to man-in-the-middle interception. Use it only against hosts you control.
warnings.plaintextHttp: This request uses http:// (not https://). Headers, body, and any credentials are sent in clear text.
warnings.credsInUrl: Credentials are embedded in the URL (user:pass@host). These can leak into logs and history; prefer a header or -u.
warnings.jsonButFormCt: The body looks like JSON, but -d does not set a JSON Content-Type. curl sends application/x-www-form-urlencoded by default, so the server may not parse it as JSON. Add -H "Content-Type: application/json".

hash-preimage-finder.name: Hash Preimage Finder
hash-preimage-finder.blurb: Watch a bounded, local brute-force search recover a weak hash input in seconds, or run out of keyspace on anything with real entropy. No wordlist, no table, just your browser. A demonstration of why fast, unsalted hashes fail.
hash-preimage-finder.hashPlaceholder: Paste an MD5, SHA-1, or SHA-256 hash
hash-preimage-finder.detectHint: Paste a hex hash; the algorithm is detected from its length.
hash-preimage-finder.notDetected: Not a recognized hash. Expected 32 (MD5), 40 (SHA-1), or 64 (SHA-256) hex characters.
hash-preimage-finder.brokenNote: cryptographically broken; never use for new work
hash-preimage-finder.keyspaceBeyond: (search stops at {cap}; larger keyspaces are the point, they are infeasible)
hash-preimage-finder.foundIn: Recovered in {attempts} attempts, {ms} ms. A secret this weak offers no protection once the hash leaks.
hash-preimage-finder.exhaustedSub: The entire keyspace was searched with no match. Widen the alphabet or length, or, more likely, the input has more entropy than a short brute force can reach.
hash-preimage-finder.budgetSub: The search hit its budget with far more keyspace remaining (this alphabet and length span {n} candidates). This is exactly why length and randomness defeat brute force: the work grows faster than any attacker can keep up with.

teach.head: This is why weak hashes fall, and how storage is actually protected
teach.intro: A fast hash of a low-entropy input is trivially reversible by enumeration, as you just saw. Real password storage defends against this in three layers.

salt.title: Salting defeats precomputed tables
salt.body: A unique random salt per password makes every stored hash different, even for identical passwords. That is what breaks lookup services like CrackStation: a precomputed table would need rebuilding for every salt, so it becomes useless.

kdf.title: Slow KDFs defeat brute force
kdf.body: bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2, and PBKDF2 are deliberately slow and tunable. Making each guess cost milliseconds instead of nanoseconds cuts an attacker's rate by six orders of magnitude or more, turning a seconds-long crack into millennia.

algo.title: Algorithm and length matter
algo.body: MD5 and SHA-1 are collision-broken and, like SHA-256, far too fast for storing secrets. Use a purpose-built password hash, and give secrets enough length and randomness that no keyspace search can reach them.

hash-preimage-finder.disclaimer: Everything runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste is uploaded. This tool exists to demonstrate hash weakness and is bounded so it can only recover trivially weak inputs.

ssrf-url-classifier.name: SSRF URL classifier
ssrf-url-classifier.blurb: Paste a URL and see where it actually points: loopback, a private or link-local range, a cloud metadata endpoint, CGNAT, reserved space, or the public internet. Decimal, octal, and hex IP obfuscation is decoded, dangerous schemes and embedded credentials are flagged, and an SSRF risk level is shown. It never resolves DNS and never sends the request.
ssrf-url-classifier.privacy: Everything is computed in your browser. This tool never resolves DNS and never sends the request; it classifies the address purely from the text you paste.

categoryDesc.loopback: The host itself (127.0.0.0/8 or ::1). A server fetching this reaches its own local services.
categoryDesc.private: An RFC 1918 private range (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16) or IPv6 ULA. Typically internal infrastructure.
categoryDesc.link-local: A link-local address (169.254.0.0/16 or fe80::/10), reachable only on the local segment.
categoryDesc.cloud-metadata: A cloud instance metadata endpoint (IMDS). Reaching it can expose credentials and instance data.
categoryDesc.cgnat: Carrier-grade NAT shared address space (100.64.0.0/10). Not public, but not classic private either.
categoryDesc.reserved: A reserved or special-use range (documentation, benchmarking, multicast, broadcast, future use).
categoryDesc.public: A public, routable address on the internet.
categoryDesc.internal-name: A hostname with an internal suffix (.internal, .local, .corp). Not a public name.
categoryDesc.unresolved: A hostname whose address is only known after a DNS lookup, which this tool does not perform.

flags.title: Flags
flags.obfuscated: The host is written in {form} form, which decodes to {ip}. This is a common way to slip an internal address past a filter that only blocks dotted-decimal.
flags.scheme: The {scheme}:// scheme is a non-HTTP fetch that escalates SSRF, for example local-file read or protocol smuggling to another service.
flags.userinfo: Credentials are embedded in the URL (user:pass@host). The real host may ignore them while a naive filter is fooled into reading the wrong part as the host.

defenses.title: Defenses
defenses.allowlist: Prefer an allow-list of exact hosts over a block-list; block-lists miss encodings and new ranges.
defenses.blockRanges: Reject loopback, private, link-local, CGNAT, and metadata ranges for both IPv4 and IPv6.
defenses.resolveThenCheck: Resolve the hostname first, then check every resulting address, to catch DNS that points at an internal IP.
defenses.noRedirects: Disable or re-validate redirects; a public URL can 302 to an internal one.
defenses.schemes: Allow only http and https; reject file, gopher, dict, ftp, and similar schemes.

ssrf-url-classifier.classifierNote: This is a classifier for learning and allow-list design. It never resolves the hostname and never sends the request, so a public hostname is reported as resolving at runtime rather than guessed.

f5-service-check-date.name: F5 service check date
f5-service-check-date.blurb: Enter a BIG-IP version to get the minimum service check date its license must carry, or enter a service check date to see the newest version you can upgrade to. Based on F5's published License Check Date table (K7727); runs entirely in your browser.
f5-service-check-date.runsLocally: This runs locally against a bundled copy of F5's License Check Date table (K7727). Nothing is sent anywhere.
f5-service-check-date.verifyNote: For production upgrade planning, always confirm against F5 K7727 or the target system's /etc/version_date file.

errors.empty: Enter a BIG-IP version or a service check date.
errors.format: That is not a BIG-IP version or a service check date. Enter a version like 17.1.3 or a date like 20230611.
errors.unknownVersion: That version is not in F5's License Check Date table.
errors.licenseNoDate: Found a Service check date label in the pasted text, but no date next to it. Check the paste or enter the date directly.

version.heading: Minimum service check date
version.forVersion: To upgrade to {label}, the license's service check date must be:
version.majorMinorNote: The check applies to the major.minor branch, so every maintenance and point release within {label} shares this date.
version.consequence: If the service check date is earlier than this, the system still boots after the upgrade but does not load its configuration until you reactivate the license.

date.heading: Upgrade eligibility
date.newestReachable: Newest version you can upgrade to
date.withinNote: Any maintenance or point release within {label} is included.
date.none: No version in the table is reachable with this service check date. Reactivate the license to obtain a newer service check date.
date.allReachable: This service check date reaches the newest version in the table; nothing is blocked.
date.blockedHeading: Newer branches you cannot reach yet
date.blockedHint: Each needs the license's service check date to be on or after the date shown. Reactivate the license first.
date.needsDate: needs a service check date of {date} or later

f5-service-check-date.pasteHint: You can also paste your /config/bigip.license contents or the tmsh show sys license output; the service check date is picked out automatically.

f5-awaf-declarative-policy-explainer.name: F5 Advanced WAF declarative-policy explainer
f5-awaf-declarative-policy-explainer.blurb: Paste a BIG-IP Advanced WAF declarative security policy (JSON) and get a section-by-section, plain-language reading grounded in F5's published schema, with security callouts that read the values: transparent enforcement means monitor-only, plus signature staging, Data Guard off, and cookies missing Secure or HttpOnly. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-declarative-policy-explainer.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser. Nothing you paste is uploaded or leaves the page.
f5-awaf-declarative-policy-explainer.empty: Paste a declarative WAF policy above to see it explained.
f5-awaf-declarative-policy-explainer.notRecognized: This does not look like a declarative WAF policy

f5-bigip-license-explainer.name: F5 BIG-IP license explainer
f5-bigip-license-explainer.blurb: Paste your /config/bigip.license, full file or a fragment, and get a plain-language reading: management flavor, licensing dates with the K7727 upgrade verdict, Registration Key and platform, active and optional modules, constraints, and feature tokens. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-bigip-license-explainer.inputPlaceholder: Paste the contents of /config/bigip.license here, the full file or any fragment.
f5-bigip-license-explainer.runsLocally: Parsed entirely in your browser. Nothing is uploaded, and key or signature values are never displayed.
f5-bigip-license-explainer.verifyNote: Field meanings follow the cited F5 articles; confirm licensing decisions against F5 documentation or support.

errors.notRecognized: That text does not look like a BIG-IP license. Expected markers such as active module, Registration Key, or the license file header.

dates.heading: Licensing dates
dates.k7727Blocked: Branches from {label} need a service check date of {date} or later, so a license reactivation would come first.

platform.heading: Platform and keys
platform.regKeyOdd: This registration key does not match the published 5-5-5-5-7 shape.
platform.decodedUnknown: Platform ID not in the vendored map; check F5 K9476, the hardware and software compatibility matrix.

modules.heading: Active modules
modules.optionalNote: Listed as licensable but dormant until licensed; provisioning may be possible while the feature stays inactive.

constraints.heading: Version and platform constraints
constraints.exvNote: Per F5 K42091606, the permitted BIG-IP range is read from the BIG-IP-style entries; F5's own example also lists low entries (5.*.* to 8.*.*) alongside them, below the K7727 table's 9.2.0 floor, without labeling what they refer to.
constraints.ceiling: Highest BIG-IP entry: {glob}. Version ceilings map to Version Plus SKU generations, per F5 K15643.

tokens.heading: Feature and limit tokens
tokens.count: {n} tokens found; values shown exactly as written.

validation.heading: Validation material
validation.privacy: Presence and length only; the values themselves are never displayed.

f5-gslb-decision-flow.name: GSLB decision-flow explainer
f5-gslb-decision-flow.blurb: The BIG-IP DNS two-tier decision, explained: pool selection at the wide IP, then the preferred, alternate and fallback chain inside the pool, with the grammar validated and the manual's rules cross-checked.
f5-gslb-decision-flow.inputLabel: gtm wideip / gtm pool stanzas, a method name, or "methods"
f5-gslb-decision-flow.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
f5-gslb-decision-flow.undocumentedToken: Not a documented token for this tier.
f5-gslb-decision-flow.poolTierTitle: Pool tier: the preferred / alternate / fallback methods

f5-topology-longest-match.name: GTM topology longest-match scorer
f5-topology-longest-match.blurb: Topology decisions computed the way BIG-IP DNS computes them: the Longest Match sort with per-record rationale, the scoring walk with shadowing shown, highest score wins, ties round-robin.
f5-topology-longest-match.inputLabel: gtm topology records (+ optional gtm region), a source line, optional candidates
f5-topology-longest-match.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
f5-topology-longest-match.candidatesTitle: Candidate scores (first matching record per candidate)
f5-topology-longest-match.sortedTitle: Records, in Longest Match sorted order

f5-dos-vector-explainer.name: AFM DoS-vector / profile explainer
f5-dos-vector-explainer.blurb: Every AFM DoS vector explained in F5's own words, with the threshold mechanics spelled out and the configuration cross-checked: the silent mitigation-below-detection inversion, automatic-mode semantics, policing without detection, and the SYN-cookie interplay. Defensive configuration only.
f5-dos-vector-explainer.inputLabel: security dos device-config / profile stanzas, a vector name, or "vectors"
f5-dos-vector-explainer.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
f5-dos-vector-explainer.defensiveNote: This tool explains defensive configuration. It never generates traffic of any kind.

f5-irules-command-context.name: iRules command / context explainer
f5-irules-command-context.blurb: Paste an iRule: every when block explained with the event's own reference one-liner, its commands inventoried and linked, the documented priority evaluation order, and a CMP audit that catches the constructs that demote your virtual server to a single TMM.
f5-irules-command-context.inputLabel: An iRule, a single event name, or "events"
f5-irules-command-context.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
f5-irules-command-context.notExecutedNote: The iRule is parsed and read, never executed.

f5-irules-vs-ltm-policy.blurb: Per when block, an honest verdict: expressible in an LTM policy (with a migration sketch in the vendor's own example grammar), verify on your version, or iRule-required with the blockers named. Policies are the no-programming layer; this tool tells you which blocks belong there.
f5-irules-vs-ltm-policy.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
f5-irules-vs-ltm-policy.sketchTitle: Policy sketch (grammar per the vendor's 12.1 examples; validate with tmsh)
f5-irules-vs-ltm-policy.precedenceTitle: Best-match precedence (head of the built-in table)

privacy_page.title: Your data, and what this site does with it
privacy_page.lede: A plain-language account of exactly what this website does and does not do with your information. It is written to be accurate rather than merely reassuring, because under regulations like the GDPR and Brazil's LGPD it is the accurate disclosure that matters.
privacy_page.short1: This site sets no cookies, runs no analytics, uses no advertising, and contains no third-party trackers or fingerprinting. Nothing follows you across the web.
privacy_page.short2: It does not sell, share, or build profiles of its visitors, and it asks you for nothing in order to read it.
privacy_page.short3: The interactive tools run entirely in your browser. What you paste or type into them stays on your device and is not transmitted anywhere.
privacy_page.short4: Only three things involve data at all, and each is explained below: a single theme setting saved in your own browser, the standard connection logs that every website visit creates at the hosting layer, and any message you choose to send by email.
privacy_page.controllerBody: This is the personal website of Rodolfo Nützmann (ronutz.com). For any privacy question, or to exercise any of the rights described below, write to {email}. Enquiries are handled directly by Rodolfo Nützmann, as the data controller.
privacy_page.noTrackTitle: No cookies, no tracking, no analytics
privacy_page.noTrackBody: This site sets no cookies of any kind. It uses no analytics service (such as Google Analytics or Plausible), no advertising network, no social-media tracking pixels, and no device fingerprinting. Your visit is not measured, profiled, or shared with anyone for those purposes.
privacy_page.browserTitle: What is stored in your browser
privacy_page.browserBody: One small thing: your chosen visual theme, saved in your browser's local storage so the site can remember it the next time you visit. It is a convenience setting that stays on your own device, is never sent to this site or to anyone else, and can be cleared at any time through your browser. No other data about you is stored on your device.
privacy_page.hostingBody: The site is delivered through Cloudflare, which provides its hosting and content delivery. As with any website, loading a page means your device connects to a server, and Cloudflare, acting as the hosting provider, processes basic connection data, including your IP address and standard request details, in order to deliver the pages to you and to protect the site against abuse and attack. This processing is necessary to operate and secure the site (a legitimate interest under GDPR Article 6(1)(f), and the corresponding bases under LGPD Articles 7 and 10). It is retained only briefly by the provider and is not used here to identify or track you.
privacy_page.toolsTitle: The tools run in your browser
privacy_page.toolsBody: The decoders and calculators on this site, for JWTs, IP addresses, certificates and the rest, compute entirely within your browser. The values you enter are processed on your own device and are never sent to a server. A separate programmatic interface (an API) is offered for automation and integrations; by design it is stateless and logs none of the queries or request bodies sent to it.
privacy_page.contactBody: The contact form does not submit to any server. Instead it opens a pre-filled message in your own email application, which you then send yourself from your own account, so nothing passes through a third party. If you do write, whether through the form or directly, I receive the information you include, such as your name, email address and message, and use it only to reply to you. I keep it only for as long as needed to deal with your enquiry, do not use it for marketing, and do not share it.
privacy_page.intlBody: Because Cloudflare operates a global network, the connection-level processing described above may take place on servers located outside your own country, including outside Brazil and the European Economic Area. Where personal data is handled internationally in this way, it is processed under the safeguards offered by the provider concerned.
privacy_page.childrenBody: This site is intended for a professional, general audience. It is not directed at children and does not knowingly collect personal data from them.
privacy_page.rightsBody: Depending on where you live, you have rights over your personal data, including to access it, to have it corrected or deleted, to object to or restrict its processing, to request a copy in a portable form, and to be told how it is handled. To exercise any of these, simply write to {email}. Because so little personal data is ever processed here, in practice there is usually very little to act on, but the channel is always open.
privacy_page.rightsGdpr: If you are in the European Economic Area or the United Kingdom, you also have the right to lodge a complaint with your local data protection supervisory authority.
privacy_page.rightsLgpd: If you are in Brazil, you hold the rights set out in Article 18 of the LGPD, and may also contact the national data protection authority (the ANPD).
privacy_page.changesBody: If this notice changes, the revised version will be published on this page and the date below will be updated. Material changes will not be applied retroactively.

about.lede: Network and security training delivered by a practitioner who has spent his career on the implementation side, not just the slides. Based in São Paulo, teaching globally, in Portuguese and English.

now.title: What I do now
now.body: I deliver official, certified instructor-led training across four security and networking platforms. There are hands-on labs on real systems, and for people who already know the fundamentals and came to practice, that is exactly the point: real environments to work in. For groups newer to a technology, the time goes first into building the foundations the topic rests on, so the hands-on work makes sense and people leave understanding why things behave the way they do, not just which buttons to press. Both are equally valuable, and the balance shifts with who is in the room. Teaching and technical enablement have been part of my work since the late 1990s, on a career that began on the implementation side in 1996.
now.teachesValue: Virtual and on-site instructor-led training, globally

platforms.title: Four platforms, taught in depth
platforms.body: Being certified across four vendors means I can teach each one properly and compare them honestly. These are the platforms I am currently authorized to deliver official training for.
platforms.f5: BIG-IP across the full instructor-led curriculum: LTM, DNS, Advanced WAF (ASM), APM, AFM, SSL Orchestrator, automation, and troubleshooting.
platforms.fortinet: Fortinet certified training, including the FCP track.
platforms.extreme: Extreme Networks switching, SD-WAN, and API and automation tracks, across installation, configuration, management, and troubleshooting.

path.title: The path here
path.intro: My career began on the vendor and implementation side in 1996, and stayed there for two decades before I focused fully on training. That implementation background is the reason the training lands: I have built, broken, and fixed these systems in production.
path.cabletron: Network engineer and certified instructor. Non-Ethernet and Ethernet networks, campus networks, IP routing.
path.riverstone: Product support engineer and knowledge-base coordinator in California. Gigabit Ethernet, metropolitan area networks, IP routing, BGP. Third-level escalation, customer scenario recreation, and regression testing.
path.cisco: Network consulting engineer, acting as the single point of contact for key customers including SERPRO and Correios. Escalation and customer-satisfaction management.
path.enterasys: Network engineer and instructor. Enterprise networks, IP routing, user access control and NAC, IDS and IPS.
path.juniper: Channel support engineer and instructor for enterprise switches and firewalls. Technical sales enablement and Junos-SRX operations training.
path.f5channel: Channel-facing sales and pre-sales engineering for F5, providing technical enablement, proof-of-concept execution, and partner training across distribution.
path.rededucation: Authorized training instructor delivering official certified courses across F5, Fortinet, Extreme Networks, and Netskope to organizations worldwide.

origins.title: Where it started
origins.body: Before the formal career, there was the curiosity. Through the early 1990s I was importing and assembling computers, running and using bulletin board systems, and working across DOS, Unix, Linux, Netware, early Ethernet, X.25, and dial-up internet. By 1995 that hands-on work had become a first formal role building an electronic-data-interchange system. The thread from then to now is the same: take something genuinely complex and make it work, then make it make sense to someone else.

approach.title: How I teach
approach.body: I chose to specialize in training because it combines deep technical knowledge with the part of the work I enjoy most: explaining complex concepts simply. The strongest sessions connect each concept to the job the learner actually has to do, so the content has somewhere to land. Real-world examples, real labs, and a focus on understanding over memorization.

cta.title: Start with the concepts
cta.body: The Learn section explains the ideas behind the tools, and the tools let you work with them directly, all in your browser.

recognition.title: Recognition
recognition.body: Recognized as an F5 DevCentral MVP for three consecutive years, in 2022, 2023, and 2024, for contributions to the F5 technical community.

credibility.certsDesc: The full list of current F5, Fortinet, Extreme Networks, and Netskope credentials, each with its issuing body and date.
credibility.endorsementsDesc: Recommendations from colleagues, partners, and students across two decades of training, quoted verbatim.
